toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. ## Developer Complaint
2. 
3. Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
4. for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
5. while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
6. 
7. We've already verified:
8. - No analyzers are slowing things down
9. - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
10. - The project itself compiles in <500ms
11. 
12. The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
13. project graph on every CLI invocation.
14. 
15. We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
16. command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.
17.

 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

data.content:
The issue is clear: `dotnet build` pays a cold-start penalty on every invocation (MSBuild process startup + project re-evaluation), while Visual Studio keeps MSBuild and the Roslyn compiler server alive between builds. Here are the fixes:

**1. Enable Shared Compilation in the project** — keeps the Roslyn compiler server (VBCSCompiler) warm between builds:

arguments.new_str:
<PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>

    <!-- Keep the Roslyn compiler server alive between builds to avoid cold-start overhead -->
    <UseSharedCompilation>true</UseSharedCompilation>
  </PropertyGroup>

data.reasoningText:
The developer is reporting that `dotnet build` from the CLI is slow for incremental builds, even when nothing has changed. They've already verified that analyzers aren't the issue and that incremental build targets work correctly. The overhead is from MSBuild process startup and project graph re-evaluation.

The key insight here is the difference between how Visual Studio builds and how `dotnet build` from CLI works:

1. **Visual Studio** keeps an MSBuild server process running in the background, so it doesn't pay the startup cost on every build.
2. **`dotnet build` from CLI** starts a new MSBuild process each time by default.

The solutions to recommend:


- **Enable the MSBuild Server** - Starting with .NET 6, there's an experimental MSBuild server feature that keeps a background process running, similar to Visual Studio. This can be activated by setting the `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1` environment variable or the `MSBuildServerEnabled` property, which eliminates the process startup overhead for subsequent builds.

- **Use `dotnet watch`** - Running `dotnet watch build` keeps the process alive and performs incremental builds automatically whenever files change, avoiding the startup cost entirely.

- **Enable MSBuild Node Reuse** - By default, `dotnet build` disables node reuse with `MSBUILDDISABLENODEREUSE=1`, which shuts down worker nodes after each build. Setting this to `0` or using `/nodeReuse:true` keeps those nodes alive between builds.

- **Enable Roslyn Compiler Server** - Using `/p:UseSharedCompilation=true` activates the VBCSCompiler process that Visual Studio relies on, keeping the compiler running persistently rather than spinning up a new instance for each build.

- **Use `dotnet watch`** for iterative development to avoid repeated startup overhead.

The real game-changer though is the MSBuild Server in .NET 8+, which you can enable with `DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1`. This keeps an MSBuild server process running between invocations, completely eliminating the startup and project graph re-evaluation that's causing the slowdown. shared compilation to keep the Roslyn compiler server running between builds, use `dotnet watch build` for faster iteration in development, and enable the MSBuild server via environment variable to eliminate startup overhead. I'll add the `UseSharedCompilation` property to the project file as the quickest win.

    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>

    <!-- Keep the Roslyn compiler server alive between builds to avoid cold-start overhead -->
    <UseSharedCompilation>true</UseSharedCompilation>
  </PropertyGroup>

data.content:
Here's the full picture of recommendations, ranked by impact:

### 1. `UseSharedCompilation` (applied above)
Keeps the VBCSCompiler server process running between builds, eliminating Roslyn startup cost. This is what Visual Studio uses by default.

### 2. Use `dotnet watch build` for the inner loop
Instead of repeatedly running `dotnet build`, use:
```
dotnet watch build
```
This keeps the MSBuild host process alive and only re-evaluates/recompiles on file changes — completely eliminating the per-invocation startup cost.

### 3. Enable the MSBuild Server
Set this environment variable in your shell profile:
```
export DOTNET_CLI_USE_MSBUILD_SERVER=1
```
This keeps an MSBuild server process running across `dotnet build` invocations, similar to how VS keeps MSBuild nodes alive. Available in .NET 8+.

### 4. Enable MSBuild Node Reuse
By default, `dotnet build` kills MSBuild worker nodes after each build. Allow them to persist:
```
export MSBUILDDISABLENODEREUSE=0
```

### Summary
| Technique | What it eliminates |
|---|---|
| `UseSharedCompilation` | Roslyn compiler cold start |
| `dotnet watch build` | CLI + MSBuild startup entirely |
| MSBuild Server | MSBuild process startup |
| Node Reuse | MSBuild worker node startup |

The combination of `UseSharedCompilation` (in the csproj) + `dotnet watch build` (workflow change) will get closest to Visual Studio's near-instant incremental rebuild experience.
